title: Taking It Big: C. Wright Mills and the Making of Political Intellectuals By Stanley Aronowitz Columbia University Press. 2012. 288 pages. $32.50 cloth
abstract: Fifty years ago, I was privileged to be among those who helped found Students for a Democratic Society (SDS), participating in the framing of its manifesto, called the Port Huron Statement. That document, drafted by 22-year-old Tom Hayden, had a number of intellectual and political influences, among which the writings of C. Wright Mills were among the most important. At the time, I was a doctoral candidate in social psychology in Ann Arbor, and Mills was my intellectual hero. Reading the Sociological Imagination early in my graduate career was a life shaping experience. Mills' “Letter to the new left,” which appeared in 1960, helped introduce the idea of a new left to American student activists; Mills, however, died just a couple of months before the June 1962 Port Huron conference—so the SDS leadership never encountered him in the flesh. But SDS in its early days had the benefit of the wisdom of several activist/intellectuals who were somewhat older than we, but very much shared our project of constructing a new left. Among the most influential and flamboyant of these was a 30-year-old union organizer named Stanley Aronowitz. He was a rare person at that point in time, having easy familiarity with the traditions and parties of the old left while being a creative and inventive organizer, with feet in both the labor and civil rights movements. He was erudite, exceptionally articulate and had been kicked out of college in the fifties—a self-made intellectual, deeply engaged in the political and, accordingly, a role model for those of us wanting to walk that path.
